coredata.dc:title: Mitochondrial specific photodynamic therapy by rare-earth nanoparticles mediated near-infrared graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dandan
Wen, Liewei
Huang, Ru
Wang, Huanhuan
Hu, Xianglong
Xing, Da
coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been proposed in cancer treatment for decades, but its clinical translation is significantly impeded by the low yield of ROS, poor tissue penetration depth of most current photosensitizers, and short lifetime of ROS. These limitations directly affect the therapeutic effect of PDT in cancer therapy. Here we proposed a new strategy by collaboratively integrating rare-earth doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNP) with graphene quantum dot (GQD) for highly efficacious PDT, based on the merits of UCNP, which can emit UV–vis light under near-infrared light (NIR) excitation, and GQD, which can produce 1O2 efficiently. For GQD-decorated UCNP nanoparticles (UCNP-GQD), the emission light from UCNP can further excite GQD with prominent 1O2 generation for NIR-triggered PDT. Furthermore, a hydrophilic rhodamine derivative, TRITC, is covalently tethered to afford the resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC, possessing distinct mitochondrial targeting property. Thus mitochondrial specific PDT with in-situ 1O2 burst in mitochondria induces sharp decrease of mitochondrial membrane potential, which initiates the tumor cell apoptosis irreversibly. Importantly, in vivo experiments demonstrate the tumor inhibition of mitochondrial targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC with improved therapeutic efficiency compared with non-targeting UCNP-GQD. The proposed strategy highlights the advantages of precision organelles-specific PDT in cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
Mitochondria targeting
Graphene quantum dots
Rare-earth upconversion nanoparticles
Singlet oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the rare-earth doped upconversion nanoparticles
Synthesis of UCNP-GQD and mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC nanoparticles
Intracellular trafficking and localization of nanoparticles
Animal tumor model and in vivo PDT treatment
In vivo biodistribution and blood circulation
Mitochondrial specific PDT-triggered mitochondrial damage and caspase 3 activation
In vivo biodistribution and blood circulation
In vivo tumor inhibition by mitochondrial specific PDT

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Guangdong Province
National Natural Scientific Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key Program of the National Natural Scientific Foundation of China (NSFC, 81630046 ), the NSFC Project of International Cooperation and Exchanges ( 61361160414 ), the NSFC Projects ( 21674040 , 51403042 ), the Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Guangdong Province ( 2016A030306013 ), the Guangdong Program for Support of Top-notch Young Professionals ( 2015TQ01R604 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2014A030310310 ), and the Scientific Research Projects of Guangzhou ( 201607010328 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical analysis and the 1O2 production potency of mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC. (a) TEM images and (b) Hydrodynamic diameter distributions by DLS analysis for GQD, UCNP and UCNP-GQD/TRITC, respectively. (c) The spectral overlap of UCNP emission and GQD absorption. (d) Upconversion luminescence spectra of GQD, UCNP, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC (λex = 980 nm), Insert: photographs of GQD, UCNP, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC upon 980 nm laser irradiation. (e) UV–vis spectra recorded for the aqueous dispersion of GQD, UCNP, TRITC, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC. (f) Decay curves of the absorption intensities of DPBF at 420 nm upon treating with nanoparticles and subsequent 980 nm laser irradiation in different duration. (g) Total laser irradiation time dependent variation of thermographic images recorded for 4T1 cells after incubation with UCNP-GQD/TRITC, the 980 nm laser treatment to cells was performed at 2 min intervals for each 1 min irradiation at a light intensity of 1.5 W/cm2. (h) Analysis of temperature changes in (g).

Mitochondria-targeting property of UCNP-GQD/TRITC and the in-situ 1O2 burst in Mitochondria. (a) CLSM images of 4T1 cells upon 5 h incubation with UCNP-GQD/TRITC (λex = 543 nm), mitochondria were stained by MitoTracker Deep Red (λex = 633 nm), and the line-scan profile in the merged image confirmed good co-localization efficiency, scale bar: 10 μm. (b) Confocal imaging of 1O2 in mitochondria captured by SOSG after different treatments. (Scale bar: 20 μm). (c) Flow cytometry analysis of the intracellular SOSG fluorescence after different treatments. (d) Statistical analysis of the SOSG fluorescence intensity in (c).

Mitochondrial specific PDT-triggered mitochondrial damage by UCNP-GQD/TRITC. (a) CLSM images of Rh123-stained cells after different treatments; Cells were pre-incubated with samples for 5 h followed by 980 nm laser irradiation. Significant fluorescence decrease of Rh123 proved the depolarization of mitochondria, scale bar: 20 μm. (b) Flow cytometry analysis of Rh123-stained cells after different treatments revealed changes ΔΨm in mitochondria. (c) CLSM images of JC-1 stained cells after different treatments. The gradual fluorescence transition from red to green in the irradiated cells indicated a loss of mitochondrial membrane potential, scale bar: 10 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The caspase 3 activity of cells upon different treatments detected by caspase 3 activity assay kit (a) in the absence and (b) in the presence of 980 nm laser irradiation. Western blot analysis of cells upon different treatments (c) in the absence and (b) in the presence of 980 nm laser irradiation.

In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of nanoparticles and the apoptosis/necrotic detection. (a) Cell viability of 4T1 cells determined by CCK-8 assay. (b) Cell apoptosis and necrosis analyzed by flow cytometer with Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining after different treatments. (c) Statistical analysis of flow cytometry data in (b).

In vivo tumor inhibition evaluation of mitochondrial specific PDT in subcutaneous xenograft tumors model. (a) Representative images of 4T1 tumor bearing mice during the treatment process. (b) Growth curves of 4T1 tumors in each group. (c) Typical photographs of tumor tissue after each treatment. (d) H&E analysis of stained tumor sections from each treating group. (e) Relative body weights of 4T1 tumor bearing mice in different groups during the whole treating period.
Typical H&E analysis of major organs after different treatments. No noticeable abnormality was found in the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney.

Schematic illustration for the fabrication of UCNP-GQD/TRITC with mitochondria-targeting potency. Upon laser irradiation, in-situ 1O2 burst in mitochondria can initiate high-efficient apoptosis of tumor cells.
Final tumor weight and tumor inhibition ratio of mice in different treatment groups.

head.ce:title: Mitochondrial specific photodynamic therapy by rare-earth nanoparticles mediated near-infrared graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science and Institute of Laser Life Science, College of Biophotonics, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510631, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science and Institute of Laser Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been proposed in cancer treatment for decades, but its clinical translation is significantly impeded by the low yield of ROS, poor tissue penetration depth of most current photosensitizers, and short lifetime of ROS. These limitations directly affect the therapeutic effect of PDT in cancer therapy. Here we proposed a new strategy by collaboratively integrating rare-earth doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNP) with graphene quantum dot (GQD) for highly efficacious PDT, based on the merits of UCNP, which can emit UV–vis light under near-infrared light (NIR) excitation, and GQD, which can produce 1O2 efficiently. For GQD-decorated UCNP nanoparticles (UCNP-GQD), the emission light from UCNP can further excite GQD with prominent 1O2 generation for NIR-triggered PDT. Furthermore, a hydrophilic rhodamine derivative, TRITC, is covalently tethered to afford the resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC, possessing distinct mitochondrial targeting property. Thus mitochondrial specific PDT with in-situ 1O2 burst in mitochondria induces sharp decrease of mitochondrial membrane potential, which initiates the tumor cell apoptosis irreversibly. Importantly, in vivo experiments demonstrate the tumor inhibition of mitochondrial targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC with improved therapeutic efficiency compared with non-targeting UCNP-GQD. The proposed strategy highlights the advantages of precision organelles-specific PDT in cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an elegant technique for oncologic intervention and therapy in modern medicine, which need three components including photosensitizer (PS), light and oxygen. When PS is excited by light in the presence of oxygen, photodynamic reactions can produce highly cytotoxic singlet oxygen (1O2) and other reactive oxygen species (ROS), which result in lesion ablation as well as the damage and death of cancer cells [1–3]. It has appeared as one of the most significant cancer treating methods due to its noninvasive nature with excellent spatiotemporal control, negligible drug resistance, low systemic toxicity, and poor side effects compared with conventional therapeutics [4–6]. However, the PDT efficiency is often limited by low 1O2 quantum yield of PS, short lifespan of ROS, and the poor tissue penetration depth of the light used to activate PS which is usually in the visible light region currently. Herein, the exploration of PS with high 1O2 quantum yield and the shift of excitation wavelength to near-infrared (NIR) range for deep tissue treatment has become a hot research topic [7,8], furthermore, to minimize the diffusion distance of ROS for lipid peroxidation, in situ organelle-specific ROS burst probably strengthen the therapeutic effect of PDT [9–12].

Recently, graphene quantum dot (GQD) are developed as the zero dimensional carbon materials including single or few layer graphene with a small size distribution [13–16]. Top-down and bottom-up strategies can be both employed to synthesize GQD [17–19]. As a new type of quantum dot, GQD have received considerable attention due to its unique properties, such as good chemical stability, excellent optical properties, outstanding electronic properties, and low toxicity [20–23]. Recently some reports have shown that GQD can be used to treat tumors based on the potency in photothermal therapy [24–26], and photodynamic therapy [27–30]. Most importantly, GQD can act as excellent electron donors as well as electron acceptors [31,32], thus acting as a good platform to produce ROS for PDT. GQD were demonstrated to produce 1O2 with a significantly high quantum yield even greater than 1.0 [28]. Furthermore, GQD not only retain graphene backbone in core region, but also possess some hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups on their surface, which can be modified with other theranostic moieties [33–36].

On the other hand, it is well-known that upconversion nanoparticles (UCNP) consisting of rare-earth element can be excited by NIR light, and convert low energy NIR light to UV–visible light [37–42], which has been widely examined in biological applications [43–48]. Long excitation wavelength of UCNP is expected to improve the insufficient tissue penetration depth and poor photostability, which is directly inaccessible by visible light [49–53]. Accordingly, if a selective PS with an excitation band matches well with the emission scope of UCNP, upon NIR light irradiation, the emitted energy can transfer from UCNP to PS to excite the PS via fluorescence resonance energy transfer, affording cytotoxic 1O2 for the killing of cancer cells [54–57]. Herein, some UCNP-based PDT systems have attracted largish attention [58–60], in which the production of 1O2 and the efficacy of PDT are critically dependent on the PDT agent used and the distance between UCNP and the PS [61–64]. However, the therapeutic efficiency is often limited by the low 1O2 quantum yield and the poor energy conversion from UCNP to the PS molecules.

Rare earth chlorides YCl3·6H2O, YbCl3·6H2O, TmCl3·6H2O, branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, 25 kDa), sodium citrate, urea, ethylene glycol, ethanol, ammonium fluoride (NH4F), NaCl, 5(6)-tetramethylrhodamine isothiocyanate (TRITC), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent. Annexin V, FITC apoptosis detection kit, and cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from Dojindo Laboratories (Kumamoto, Japan). Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) and MitoTracker Deep Red 633 were purchased from Invitrogen life technologies. Caspase 3 Activity Assay Kit was purchased from Beyotime. Cy7.5 NHS ester was purchased from Seebio Biotech (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. All reagents were used without further purification. Water used in this work was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system with an electric resistance of 18.4 MΩ cm.

GQD nanoparticles were synthesized according to previous methods [14,15,18]. Typically, citric acid (0.21 g) and urea (0.18 g) were dissolved into water (5 mL), then the solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 4 h in a heated thermostatic magnetic stirrer. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the product was lyophilized in a freeze dryer. The obtained solid can be easily re-suspended in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the rare-earth doped upconversion nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The rare-earth doped nanoparticles were synthesized via a solvothermal method with slight modification [60]. Typically, deionized water (2 mL), Y (0.595 mmol), Yb (0.4 mmol), Tm (0.005 mmol), NaCl (1 mmol) were mixed under stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Then, deionized water (3 mL) containing PEI (1 g) were added to modify UCNP nanoparticles with amino groups. After 20 min stirring, ethylene glycol (20 mL) were added slowly. Then NH4F (0.20 g) in water (5 mL) were added to the above solution. After 30 min stirring, the final solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, which was heated at 160 °C for 12 h, then cooling down, the rare-earth nanoparticles were afforded by centrifugation, repeated washing with ethanol and water for several times, and final drying under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of UCNP-GQD and mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To covalently conjugate GQD to UCNP, DMF (5 mL) containing GQD (0.5 mg), EDC (1 mg), and NHS (1 mg) were stirred at room temperature for 2 h, and then amino-functionalized UCNP (0.5 mg) was added into the solution and stirred vigorously for 24 h. The mixture solution was then centrifuged, and the precipitate was washed with water to remove any unreacted GQD to afford UCNP-GQD. The loading content of GQD was calculated according to the absorption spectrum of GQD. For TRITC-decorated UCNP-GQD/TRITC, a similar process was taken, the carboxyl group of TRITC was activated by NHS, then added in the dispersion of UCNP-GQD. After centrifugation and washing with water to remove any free TRITC and subsequent dialysis against water, the resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC nanoparticles were afforded.
Mouse mammary tumor cell line 4T1 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 (GIBCO), supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum (FCS) in 5% CO2, 95% air at 37 °C in a humidified incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular trafficking and localization of nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular trafficking of UCNP-GQD/TRITC in 4T1 cells was evaluated by staining mitochondria with MitoTracker Deep Red 633. Cells were seeded on confocal dish and cultured for 24 h with normal culture medium. Then, the culture medium was replaced with a new medium containing UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC. After incubating for 5 h, the cells were washed three times with PBS, then stained with MitoTracker Deep Red for 20 min, and washed three times with PBS. Finally, the fluorescent images were detected by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with the excitation wavelengths set at 543 nm and 633 nm for TRITC moieties within UCNP-GQD/TRITC and MitoTracker Deep Red, respectively.

To assess the 1O2 generating capability of UCNP-GQD/TRITC, 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a detection probe. A 980 nm laser beam with an average power of 1.5 W/cm2 was used for the irradiation experiments. The determination of 1O2 can be evaluated by the absorption decrease at 420 nm for DPBF. On the other hand, the intracellular 1O2 generation was detected by SOSG, which is highly sensitive to 1O2, but does not show any appreciable response to hydroxyl radical or superoxide. Upon incubation with nanoparticles, 4T1 cells were labelled with SOSG for 20 min, washed three times with PBS. After that, the cells were exposed to 980 nm irradiation for 8 min at a power density of 1.5 W/cm2, then the cells were washed with PBS to remove free dye or nanoparticles. The fluorescence imaging of cells was recorded by CLSM with the excitation wavelength set at 514 nm. Please note that, the 980 nm laser treatment to cells was performed at 2 min intervals for each 1 min irradiation to prevent the potential heating effect of laser light during the illumination process. A 980 nm laser irradiated the entire area of culture dish at room temperature, and the temperature of cells in the culture medium was monitored at the interval time points during the irradiation process by a Thermal Imager, exhibiting unobvious temperature increasing.

In order to assess the mitochondrial membrane potential, Rh123 (5 mmol) was employed as a fluorescent indicator. After incubation with samples and subsequent NIR laser illumination, 4T1 cells were washed three times with PBS and stained with Rh123 for 20 min. After washing three times with PBS, the CLSM images were recorded with the excitation wavelength set at 488 nm. All the parameters used in the confocal microscopy were kept constant for each sample. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was also evaluated by flow cytometry (FACS) with the excitation wavelength set at 488 nm. The value of fluorescence intensity was analyzed by the FCS Express Version 3.0 software. To evaluate the decrease of mitochondrial membrane potential convincingly, JC-1 staining was also employed to prove this change via the transition from red fluorescence to green fluorescence when the mitochondrial membrane potential decreases.

The activity of caspase 3 in 4T1 cells was assessed by the specific protease-peptide substrate chromogenic reaction. Typically, 4T1 cells cultured in 6-well plates at 4 × 105 cells/well were treated with different treatments, including fresh culture medium (as control), culture medium containing UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC, fresh culture medium with 980 nm laser irradiation and culture medium containing UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC with 980 nm laser irradiation. After that, the cells were harvested, lysed, and centrifuged. Then, aliquots of the supernatants were collected and incubated with the peptide substrates of caspase 3, Ac-DEVD-ρNA, respectively. The catalytic product ρNA has a strong absorption at ∼405 nm. Herein the activity of caspase 3 was determined based on the absorbance at 405 nm by a microplate reader. The relative activity ratio of caspase 3 was calculated as compared with the blank control. Each measurement was conducted with three independent experiments.

In order to authoritatively prove that caspase 3 is activated, western blot analysis is necessary. Typically, before the cells were harvested, all the treatments and conditions were the same as above. Then, the lysates preparation and western blot analysis were performed as that described in the literature. 60 μg protein lysate was subjected to SDS-PAGE using a 15% gel, transferred to PVDF membranes, then incubated with designated primary antibody (rabbit monoclonal anti-caspase-3 antibody and mouse monoclonal anti-β-actin antibody) and secondary antibody (goat anti-rabbit IgG and goat anti-mouse IgG). Finally, the signals were recorded by an Odyssey infrared imaging system.

Cell viability was examined according to the CCK-8. Cells were seeded into 96-well plate (100 μL per well). After 24 h culture, 100 μL fresh medium containing UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC was added to each well with the content from 0 to 160 μg/mL, six parallel wells for each concentration were performed. Before being exposed to NIR irradiation, the cells were incubated for another 12 h at 37 °C, and then washed three times with PBS. An adjustable 980 nm laser were used to illuminate the 96-well plate for 8 min at 1.5 W/cm2 at 2 min intervals for each 1 min irradiation to avoid thermal effects. Then the cells were incubated for additional 24 h. After changing with fresh medium, 10 μL CCK-8 solution and 90 μL fresh medium was added to each well and the cells were incubated for 30 min. Then the absorbance intensities at 450 nm were recorded with a plate reader to determine the cell viability.

4T1 cells were plated into a 6-well plate and culture for 24 h. Then the medium containing UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC take place of the normal medium for further 5 h incubation. After washed three times with PBS, the 6-well plate were exposured to an adjustable 980 nm laser for 8 min at 1.5 W/cm2 at 2 min intervals for each 1 min irradiation. Then the cells were incubated for additional 24 h. Cells were harvested after each treatment and stained with Annexin-V-FITC and PI, then analyzed by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animal tumor model and in vivo PDT treatment
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells (2 × 106) in PBS solution (100 μL) were subcutaneously injected into the flank region of female Balb/c mice, aged 4–5 weeks. Mice bearing with 4T1 tumors were used in experiments when the tumors reached a size of approximately 50 mm3. The tumor-bearing mice were randomly assigned into six groups (n = 10): (1) control (only injected with saline); (2) UCNP-GQD injection alone; (3) UCNP-GQD/TRITC injection alone; (4) 980 nm laser irradiation alone (0.5 W/cm2, 20 min); (5) UCNP-GQD injection + 980 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 20 min); (6) UCNP-GQD/TRITC injection + 980 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 20 min). To avoid any potential heating effect, the laser treatment was performed with a 2 min interval for each 1 min irradiation. The therapeutic efficacy was assessed by measuring the tumor volume, body weight in each group every four days in the duration of 20 days. Tumor volume was calculated as length × (width)2/2. Relative tumor volume and relative body weight of mice were calculated as V/V0, and W/W0, respectively. (V0 and W0 were the corresponding tumor volume and body weight respectively when the treatment was initiated). The mice were killed and the tumors were collected after treatments at 20 days. Tumor inhibition rate (%) = ((Wc-Wt)/Wc) × 100, where Wc and Wt are the average tumor weight of control group and treatment group, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and blood circulation
ce:section.ce:para: After molting with depilatory totally, tumor bearing mice were injected intravenously with Cy7.5-labelled UCNP-GQD/TRITC and imaged at different time points (0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 9 h, 12 h, 24 h) with an infrared imaging system (Odyssey LI-COR, USA). To observe the biodistribution in tissue, mice were sacrificed at 24 h post-injection, and the major organs and tumors were collected and imaged. Blood circulation was measured by drawing 15 μL blood from the tail vein after intravenous injection at different time points. Then each blood sample was dissolved in 135 μL of Lysis buffer (1% Sodium Dodecyl Sulfonate, 1% Triton X-100, 40 mM Tris acetate). Finally, the Cy7.5 fluorescence was recorded by the microplate reader.

To confirm the therapeutic efficacy, the mice were sacrificed after each treatment for the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) analysis. Organs and tumor tissues from all the groups were excised, fixed in 4% formalin overnight, and then embedded in paraffin. The sliced tissue sections were stained with standard H&E staining and then analyzed with a microscope.

Current UCNP nanoparticles with the composition of NaYF4:59.5% Y3+, 40% Yb3+, and 0.5% Tm3+ were coated with branched polyethylenimine (PEI), in which the amine groups of PEI were employed to react with the carboxyl groups on GQD nanoparticles. After that, TRITC was further modified on UCNP-GQD. The size of GQD and UCNP was relatively uniform based on the TEM analysis, at a diameter of ∼6 nm and ∼80 nm, respectively. The resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC exhibited a diameter of ∼100 nm with distinctive low contrast edge around the high-contrast UCNP core (Fig. 1 a). Comparable results about the hydrodynamic size distribution were determined by dynamic light scattering measurement (Fig. 1b). In addition, the dispersion color change in the preparation process confirmed the successful fabrication of UCNP-GQD/TRITC (Fig. S1). There is no noteworthy difference for the aqueous dispersion of UCNP, GQD, and free TRITC, after centrifugation, whereas the almost colorless supernatant of UCNP-GQD/TRITC verified the successful covalent modification of GQD and TRITC on the surface of UCNP.

Furthermore, FT-IR spectra of TRITC, GQD, UCNP, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC were recorded respectively (Fig. S2). For PEI-functionalized UCNP, the peak around 3400 cm−1 was attributed to the N-H stretching vibration within amine groups, confirming the successful PEI coating on UCNP. The signals at 1335 cm−1, 1560 cm−1 and 3180 cm−1 for GQD corresponded to the aromatic C–O, CC,OH, stretching peaks respectively. After surface covalent modification with GQD and subsequent TRITC conjugation, the resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC nanoparticles exhibited new generated signals from amide groups at ∼1635 cm−1, and other peaks from TRITC moieties were also present, further verifying the successful covalent fabrication.

In addition, Power X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the nanoparticles were obtained from a D8 Focus diffractometer (Bruker) with use of Cu K α radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm) in the 2θ range from 10° to 70°. As shown in Fig. S3, the XRD result of UCNP nanoparticles could be ascribed to a mixture of the phases NaYF4 (JCPDS. 77-2042) and NaYbF4 (JCPDS. 27-1427), with the former as the core phase. For GQD, the wide range peak at 30° demonstrated its amorphous phase structure. Furthermore, PEI-coated UCNP nanoparticles were positively charged, +14.9 mV, which was due to the presence of amine groups in PEI moieties, while the zeta potential of GQD was determined to be −12.0 mV, because of carboxyl groups on the surface. After decoration, the zeta potential of UCNP-GQD becomes slightly positive, +2.33 mV. It was much less than that of PEI-coated UCNP, because the fabrication of UCNP with GQD would consume/mask part of the surface amine groups, thus confirming the successful formation of UCNP-GQD. In addition, the conjugation of positively-charged TRITC could increase the positive charge, up to +7.56 mV for UCNP-GQD/TRITC (Fig. S4).

GQD nanoparticles showed a broad absorption spectrum with a maximum absorption peak at ∼340 nm, which matched well with emission peaks of UCNP at 336 nm and 363 nm upon irradiation by a 980 nm laser (Fig. 1c). In addition, the visible emission peaks of UCNP at 445 nm, and 470 nm were also covered by the absorption region of GQD. To confirm the potential energy transfer from UCNP to the decorated GQD, the emission spectra of GQD, UCNP, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC were determined with the excitation wavelength set at 980 nm (Fig. 1d). The emission intensity of UCNP-GQD/TRITC was significantly quenched by GQD to produce cytotoxic 1O2 potentially. Particularly, the emission intensity near UV region was almost completely disappeared due to the strong absorption of GQD at ∼340 nm, and the emission at ∼445 nm and ∼470 nm were also partially quenched. The FRET efficiency from UCNP to GQD, determined from the quench as E = (I0 − I1)/I0, where I0 and I1 are the emission intensities of UCNP and UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC, respectively. The FRET efficiencies of UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC were compared at different emission peaks; specially, 91.1% vs 85.1% at 336 nm, 75.7% vs 78.7% at 363 nm, 54.0% vs 49.0% at 445 nm, 45.1% vs 44.8% at 470 nm, respectively. Herein, it indicated that TRITC had little effect on the energy transfer from UCNP to GQD. Photographs of UCNP, UCNP-GQD and UCNP-GQD/TRITC under 980 nm laser irradiation clearly showed that the incorporation of GQD would partially quench the emission of UCNP (Fig. 1d inset). It should be noted that pure GQD doesn't possess upconversion emission property. The UV emission peaks can be derived from radioactive transitions of 1I6 -3F4 (336 nm) and 1D2 -3H6 (363 nm), and the visible emission can be attributed to 1D2-3F4 (445 nm) and 1G4-3H6 (470 nm) transitions of Tm3+ ions, respectively [11].

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of UCNP-GQD/TRITC was further analyzed, in which the characteristic peaks of GQD and TRITC appeared definitely (Fig. 1e). The loading capacity of GQD in UCNP-GQD/TRITC was determined by calculating the characteristic absorption peak of GQD based on the standard curve (Fig. S5), exhibiting significantly high loading of GQD in UCNP-GQD/TRITC, up to ∼18.8 wt%, which was the premise for high 1O2 production.

1O2 is highly cytotoxic, rapidly attacking any organic compounds it encounters in PDT, which can kill cancer cells via the activation of cell apoptosis or necrosis. Then the 1O2 production capability of UCNP-GQD/TRITC was interrogated by a commercial fluorescent probe, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), which reacts irreversibly with 1O2, resulting in the absorbance decrease of DPBF. Upon 980 nm NIR laser illumination in the duration from 0 to 5 min at 1.5 W/cm2, similar level of absorbance decrease of DPBF was detected upon treating with UCNP-GQD/TRITC or UCNP-GQD (Fig. 1f, Fig. S6). In addition, longer the illumination time was, much faster the absorbance intensity decreased. The slopes of these curves represented the efficiency of 1O2 generation with the extension of irradiation time. Both UCNP and GQD had minimal effect on the absorbance of DPBF, which further conformed that 1O2 could be efficiently generated from UCNP-GQD/TRITC and UCNP-GQD after NIR light irradiation and subsequent energy transfer process.

It is worth noting that the potential heating effect of 980 nm laser irradiation may affect the experimental results. In order to evaluate whether cells treated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC and concurrent laser irradiation could be heated, the temperature and thermographic images of 4T1 cells were recorded, exhibiting minimal temperature increasing (Fig. 1g). During the illumination process, the laser irradiated the entire area of culture dish at room temperature, and the laser treatment was performed at 2 min intervals for each 1 min irradiation to avoid the photothermal effect. The temperature was monitored during the alternant irradiation process, exhibiting slight rising of ∼2.2 °C for total 10 min irradiation (Fig. 1h).

The presence of TRITC endowed UCNP-GQD/TRITC with potential mitochondria-targeting potency, which was observed by CLSM imaging (Fig. 2 a). Mitochondria were stained with Mitotracker Deep Red to co-localize the nanoparticles. The green fluorescence from UCNP-GQD/TRITC was found to match well with the red fluorescent pixels of mitochondria. The overlay yellow fluorescence indicated that UCNP-GQD/TRITC could selectively accumulate in mitochondria due to the distinctive mitochondria targeting potency, which was definitely confirmed by the line scan analysis.

After confirming the mitochondrial targeting of UCNP-GQD/TRITC, the intracellular 1O2 generation was detected by SOSG, which was highly sensitive to 1O2 and does not show any appreciable response to hydroxyl radical or superoxide (Fig. 2b). SOSG emitted green fluorescence in the presence of 1O2, and the intracellular fluorescence intensity of SOSG was proportional to the amount of 1O2. Without 980 nm laser irradiation, the green fluorescence channel of SOSG was unobvious for cells treated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC or UCNP-GQD. However, upon laser irradiation, cells treated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC or UCNP-GQD exhibited strong green fluorescence in SOSG channel compared with the blank. Most importantly, cells treated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC and laser irradiation, the green fluorescent pixels of SOSG co-localized distinctively with the red mitochondria by the line-scan analysis, while the SOSG fluorescence in the group of UCNP-GQD with laser irradiation randomly distributed in the whole cell. Herein, the greatly enhanced fluorescence intensity of SOSG and the colocalization analysis revealed that UCNP-GQD/TRITC could specially produce high-yield 1O2 in mitochondria after NIR laser illumination.

After that, the intracellular mitochondria-specific 1O2 generation was further determined by flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 2c and d). Compared with other groups, when the cells were incubated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC or UCNP-GQD, and subjected to appropriate 980 nm laser, the fluorescent intensity of SOSG increased greatly, whereas subsequent addition of vitamin C significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity due to the quenching effect of vitamin C on 1O2 (Fig. 2c). Further statistical analysis confirmed the result quantitatively (Fig. 2d). The 1O2 generating capability for UCNP-GQD/TRITC and UCNP-GQD was in similar level, no matter the diversity of intracellular localization, which was consistent with the results obtained in above experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitochondrial specific PDT-triggered mitochondrial damage and caspase 3 activation
ce:section.ce:para: The decrease of mitochondrial membrane potential was demonstrated to imply the mitochondrial dysfunction [69,74,75]. To investigate the mitochondrial targeting PDT potency of UCNP-GQD/TRITC, cells from all treating groups were stained with rhodamine 123 (Rh123) dye, which was a fluorescent probe of mitochondrial membrane potential, and the change of Rh123 fluorescence signals was analyzed by CLSM imaging and FACS. For the group of UCNP-GQD/TRITC with 980 nm laser treating, the Rh123 signal significantly decreased compared to that of other groups, indicating a serious decrease of mitochondria membrane potential (Fig. 3 a). Further FACS analysis of Rh123-stained cells were convinced to verify the fluorescence decrease of Rh123 and dysfunction of mitochondrial membrane in the group of UCNP-GQD/TRITC and UCNP-GQD with light treating (Fig. 3b), in which the dysfunction extent of mitochondria membrane was much stronger for mitochondrial targeting UCNP- GQD/TRITC compared with non-targeting UCNP-GQD. Furthermore, JC-1 staining was well consistent with the previous results due to the presence of green fluorescence, proving the reduced mitochondria membrane potential when the cells was incubated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC and irradiated with 980 nm laser (Fig. 3c). Herein, the cytotoxic 1O2 generated specifically in mitochondria resulted in instant 1O2 burst and mitochondrial damage, which could overcome the depletion and elimination of short-life 1O2 during its outward diffusion if it was not in-situ formed in mitochondria [76].

Additionally, the activity of caspase 3 was known as the main effector of apoptotic pathways, then it was evaluated by a caspase 3 activity assay kit. Without 980 nm irradiation, the activity level of both UCNP-GQD/TRITC and UCNP-GQD was comparable with the blank group (Fig. 4 a), suggesting unnoticeable activity of caspase 3, whereas upon irradiating with the laser light, ∼3.6-fold dramatic increase was observed for UCNP-GQD/TRITC. Meanwhile, ∼2.5-fold enhancement was found for UCNP-GQD (Fig. 4b), further highlighting the advantage of mitochondria targeting PDT based on UCNP-GQD/TRITC. Finally, western blot analysis was performed to demonstrate the activation of caspases 3 for cells upon incubation with nanoparticles and illumination, but not when there was no laser irradiation (Fig. 4c and d). These consistent results proposed that 1O2 instantly produced in mitochondria could activate caspase 3 efficiently, and eventually initiate the programmed cell apoptosis.

Subsequently, CCK-8 assay was employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of UCNP-GQD/TRITC. The cytotoxicity of non-targeted UCNP-GQD and mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC without irradiation was evaluated, and negligible cytotoxicity were exhibited against 4T1 cells, with >90% cell viability at a content even up to 160 μg/mL, which suggested that current nanoparticles had good biocompatibility (Fig. 5 a). This also indicated that the nanoparticles have poor toxicity to the cells in the dark. On the other hand, tumor cells were incubated with UCNP-GQD/TRITC for 5 h, followed by irradiation with a 980 nm laser, the cell viability declined apparently compared with light-free groups, and more importantly, mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC exhibited higher cytotoxicity than non-targeted UCNP-GQD, which highlighted the design superiority of mitochondria targeting PDT. These results demonstrated that the mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC had excellent dark compatibility and superior photodynamic therapeutic efficacy to tumor cells compared with non-targeted UCNP-GQD.

Furthermore, to clarify the therapeutic mechanism and cell death mode induced by mitochondria targeting PDT, the cell apoptosis and necrosis after drug administration were analyzed by FACS with Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining, which could clearly distinguish apoptosis from necrosis. (Fig. 5b and c). Without 980 nm laser illumination, neither UCNP-GQD nor UCNP-GQD/TRITC could induce apparent cell death, because the cells showed a large viable cell population (>87%) with very few staining for apoptotic and necrotic cells. Upon 980 nm laser treating, UCNP-GQD or UCNP-GQD/TRITC displayed a stronger shift from live cells to apoptotic cell populations; specifically, the apoptotic ratio of mitochondria-targeting UCNP-GQD/TRITC was significantly higher than that of nonspecific UCNP-GQD, ∼61.0% vs ∼30.8%. These results indicated that apoptosis was a major cell death modality in current work, and UCNP-GQD/TRITC conserved the outstanding potency of mitochondria-targeting PDT, which triggered high-efficient apoptosis via the mitochondria damage-induced programmed cell apoptosis for tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution and blood circulation
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the pharmacokinetics of nanoparticles, UCNP-GQD/TRITC nanoparticles were labelled with Cy7.5 via the remaining amine groups with Cy7.5 NHS ester for in vivo fluorescence imaging. The in vivo biodistribution of Cy7.5-labelled UCNP-GQD/TRITC in 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c mice was directly observed by an infrared imaging system. Upon 3 h post-injection, obvious fluorescence was found at the tumor sites, while the fluorescent signal in the liver weakened (Fig. S7a). The fluorescent signals in tumor tissue strengthened with the time extension, and the quantitative fluorescence intensity of tumor was much higher than other major organs at 24 h post-injection (Fig. S7b-c). These results indicated that the nanoparticles could effectively accumulate in tumor sites via the well-accepted EPR effect, and the long-term accumulation in tumor sites was preferable than major organs. Furthermore, blood was taken from the treated mice at different time points upon injection of Cy7.5-labelled UCNP-GQD/TRITC, and the blood circulation half-life was determined to be ∼2.5 h (Fig. S7d). The covalent linking of different components in the nanoparticle was favorable for the structural integrity and blood circulation stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor inhibition by mitochondrial specific PDT
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo therapeutic efficacies of mitochondrial specific PDT based on UCNP-GQD/TRITC were evaluated by intravenous treatment of mice bearing subcutaneous 4T1 tumors in 20 days. The mice were divided into six groups with 10 mice per group: PBS, UCNP-GQD, UCNP-GQD/TRITC, only 980 nm laser irradiation, UCNP-GQD with 980 nm laser and the resultant UCNP- GQD/TRITC with 980 nm. The tumor volumes and mouse body weights were measured periodically during the treatment process. The tumor volume increased remarkably which had a rapid tumor growth up to ∼11-fold at day 20 in the first four groups.

In addition, H&E analysis revealed that the group of UCNP-GQD/TRITC exhibited much more pyknotic cells with highly condensed nuclei, which were considered to be apoptotic or dead cells (Fig. 6d). The high in vivo antitumor therapeutic efficacy most probably resulted from the integrated advantages of UCNP-GQD/TRITC, including high-efficient yield of 1O2, and in-situ 1O2 generation in mitochondria, affording severe damage to mitochondria and improved therapeutic efficacy.

The body weight of mice in six treating groups had no significant changes during the whole treatment process, suggesting unobvious systematic toxicity (Fig. 6e). In addition, there was no obvious pathological changes of the main organs in UCNP-GQD/TRITC treated group, indicating undetectable systemic toxicity (Fig. 7 ). The favorable biocompatibility of UCNP-GQD/TRITC in the dark was beneficial for reducing side effects but not sacrificing the therapeutic efficacy, which further highlighted the advantages of the nanoparticles. Herein in vitro and in vivo exploration demonstrated that it was highly promising to employ current mitochondria specific PDT for tumor treatments.

In conclusion, a novel therapeutic platform, UCNP-GQD/TRITC, was developed based on the upconversion nanoparticle mediated graphene quantum dots for mitochondria-targeting tumor photodynamic therapy, which could generate 1O2 in mitochondria specifically and trigger the dysfunction of mitochondria to initiate programmed cell apoptosis. Upon irradiation by NIR laser light, UCNP-GQD/TRITC could produce high dosage of 1O2 due to the high efficient energy transfer from UCNP to GQD as well as the intrinsic advantages of GQD. Furthermore, the mitochondrial targeting potency of UCNP-GQD/TRITC endowed the platform with much higher therapeutic efficacy compared with non-targeted UCNP-GQD, which was demonstrated by deliberate in vitro and in vivo explorations. The resultant UCNP-GQD/TRITC were biocompatible in the dark, whereas exhibiting high tumor suppression potency with an inhibition ratio of >75% upon NIR light treatments. Therefore, the strategy of mitochondrial targeting PDT is promising for the noninvasive treatment of deep tumors and persistent tumors in precision cancer therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Program of the National Natural Scientific Foundation of China (NSFC, 81630046), the NSFC Project of International Cooperation and Exchanges (61361160414), the NSFC Projects (21674040, 51403042), the Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of Guangdong Province (2016A030306013), the Guangdong Program for Support of Top-notch Young Professionals (2015TQ01R604), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2014A030310310), and the Scientific Research Projects of Guangzhou (201607010328).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2017.10.034.
